intro:
Debt is one of the biggest obstacles to financial freedom, and knowing exactly how long it will take to pay off is the first step to eliminating it. Our debt payoff calculator shows you precisely how many months remain on any loan or credit card balance, the total amount you will pay, and how much of that is interest.

The calculator works for any fixed-rate debt: credit cards, personal loans, student loans, auto loans, or any revolving balance. Simply enter your current balance, annual interest rate, and your planned monthly payment to see your complete payoff timeline.

Understanding the true cost of debt can be a powerful motivator. A $5,000 credit card balance at 20% APR paid off at $100 per month takes 94 months and costs $4,311 in interest — nearly doubling the original debt. Increasing that payment to $200 per month reduces the time to 32 months and slashes interest to $1,320.
howItWorks: Enter your current outstanding balance, annual interest rate (APR), and your planned monthly payment. The calculator simulates each month's payment — applying interest first, then reducing principal — and counts the months until the balance reaches zero. It totals all payments and subtracts the original balance to show total interest paid.
interpretationGuide: A shorter payoff timeline and lower total interest are always better. Even small increases in monthly payment dramatically reduce both time and total cost. Use this calculator to find the payment amount that lets you pay off debt within your target timeline.

faqs.question: Why must my monthly payment exceed the monthly interest?
faqs.answer: If your payment does not cover the monthly interest charge, the remaining balance actually grows each month — a situation called negative amortization. To make progress on debt, your payment must exceed (Balance × Annual Rate ÷ 12).

faqs.question: How much extra should I pay to save significantly on interest?
faqs.answer: Even adding $25–$50 per month above the minimum can save hundreds or thousands in interest and years of payments. The impact is greatest early in the loan when the balance is highest and most of each payment goes toward interest.

faqs.question: What is the debt avalanche method?
faqs.answer: The debt avalanche method prioritizes paying off high-interest debts first while making minimum payments on others. Mathematically, this minimizes total interest paid and is the most cost-efficient strategy for multiple debts.

faqs.question: What is the debt snowball method?
faqs.answer: The debt snowball method pays off the smallest balance first regardless of interest rate, building psychological momentum. While it may cost slightly more interest than the avalanche method, it provides motivational wins that help many people stay on track.

faqs.question: Should I invest or pay off debt first?
faqs.answer: Generally, pay off high-interest debt (above 6–7%) before investing, since credit card rates of 15–25% are far higher than typical investment returns. For low-interest debt (under 4%), investing may be mathematically better. Employer 401(k) matches are always worth capturing first.

faqs.question: What is APR vs. interest rate on a credit card?
faqs.answer: APR (Annual Percentage Rate) includes both the interest rate and any fees, expressed as a yearly rate. For credit cards, APR and interest rate are typically the same. For mortgages and auto loans, APR may be higher than the interest rate because it includes origination fees.

faqs.question: How does the calculator handle the final payment?
faqs.answer: The calculator simulates month-by-month amortization. In the final month, the payment is adjusted to exactly equal the remaining balance plus last month's interest, which is why total paid may differ slightly from payment × months.

faqs.question: Can I use this for credit card debt?
faqs.answer: Yes, this calculator works perfectly for credit card debt. Enter your current balance, APR (on your statement), and your planned fixed monthly payment. Note that credit cards allow minimum payments that can leave you in debt for decades — this calculator shows why paying more matters so much.

faqs.question: What happens if I miss a payment?
faqs.answer: Missing a payment adds interest for an extra month and typically triggers a late fee, worsening the total cost. Many credit cards also have penalty APR clauses that can raise your rate to 29.99% after missed payments. Automating minimum payments prevents accidental misses.

faqs.question: How does balance transfer affect debt payoff?
faqs.answer: A balance transfer to a 0% intro APR card can dramatically accelerate debt payoff because every payment reduces principal during the promotional period (typically 12–21 months). Watch for transfer fees (usually 3–5% of balance) and ensure you can pay it off before the promotional rate expires.
